The quickest workaround is to just copy `actor2.py` to worker nodes.

`ray.broadcast_module` sounds like a good idea, especially when people only want to run quick scripts, they don't need to manually copy files.

But for large-scale projects, I think the best way is still copying code or deploying code via a wheel. Otherwise, we'll end up with doing a ton of `broadcast_module`s.

Regarding taking care of everything under the hood automatically, I doubt if it's even feasible. Because Python is too dynamic to detect all dependencies beforehand. (An alternative way is to go back to driver when something is missing, but that may introduce considerable overhead, and make the system too complex.)

With this configuration, we have 1 object store, but 2 raylets registered to the GCS as distinct clients. How does the python client decide where to submit tasks?

Assuming a round-robin scheme WRT which raylet is used: It's possible that task `X` executes on raylet `A`, registers the object's location with GCS, while raylet `B` attempts to pull that object. Of course, when raylet `A` pushes the object to raylet `B`, the call to create the object in the object store fails, since it's already there.

One possible fix might involve introducing a node id, where raylets on the same node have the same node id. This would be distinct from the client id, and would be used instead of the client id to prevent a raylet from pulling an object to the node it's running on.

Another, simpler, possible fix (if the semantics work out) could be to use the same client id for raylets on the same node. However, I'm not a fan of this solution as the name `ClientID` no longer uniquely identifies a raylet's GCS client.

I'm not familiar with the configuration of using multiple raylets on a single node. @atumanov does this configuration make sense for xray?

Although this is unexpected behavior, I think it's low priority since duplicate transfers are non-fatal.

The constants can be added by **prepending** them to the [`modernui`](https://github.com/takluyver/pynsist/blob/1.9/nsist/pyapp.nsi#L14-L28) block.
```
!define MUI_FINISHPAGE_NOAUTOCLOSE

```

WriteRegStr HKLM "Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\${PRODUCT_NAME}" "DisplayVersion" "..."

Is it even pip's job to create man pages? If you're using the `--user` parameter, there's no way for pip to install man pages in the `manpath`, as those man paths are usually configured only system-wide and are user independent. And even if pip is used for globally installing packages (which is bad and will result in file conflicts sooner or later), it is not its job to write files in one of the system man paths.
I'd say that the proper distro packages need to be installed for having man pages.

If we added a new parameter `--url` (or something) that could be used instead of the usual `URL` argument then you could just use the existing config loading syntax (`@config.cfg`). The config loader uses the same syntax as the main `streamlinkrc` file. Then you would be able to do `streamlink @bookmark.cfg` (with `--url` and `--default-stream`, plus any other options you need.)

> What about not supporting passing configuration options directly to `run()` at all? Instead, if non-default behavior is desired, the caller can instantiate, say, a `TrioRunner` object with those options and use `runner.run()`.

Interesting idea! And for `trio.run` alone, this would probably work. But, the same problem happens for:

* `trio.run`
* `nursery.start_soon`
* `nursery.start`
* `trio.run_sync_in_worker_thread`
* `trio.BlockingTrioPortal.run`
* `trio.BlockingTrioPortal.run_sync`
* `trio.hazmat.spawn_system_task`
* `trio.hazmat.TrioToken.run_sync_soon`

And then trio-asyncio has the same problem for its functions to call trio-from-asyncio and vice-versa, and I'm sure this will keep cropping up around the ecosystem.

Adding a configobject interface for all of these seems really cumbersome? And I'd rather have just one solution to this problem that gets used everywhere, so you only have to learn it once, rather than two different ones so then you have to learn both solutions, *and* which solution is used where.

Sounds good! Were you guys thinking a testing mode configuration flag, or an automated filter? Everyone makes mistakes, so if we let it be possible for us to forget, I think we will forget from time to time.

Filter pros:
1) Eliminates per-workflow possibility dev forgets to set the flag

Filter cons:
1) Introduces per-dev possibility we forget to add dev to the filter
2) Possible collisions (user gets filtered as dev)-- probably unlikely for now
3) Less flexible: there may be situations where devs should count as users
4) More work for new people to contribute

OK, maybe I'm overthinking this, but maybe filter+config flag is the way to go. In other words, if the config flag is not set, the default would be to evaluate the filter (so devs would by default run in testing mode). But the configuration flag would override that so that a dev could run in user mode, or a user could run in testing mode. That would eliminate cons 3) and 4).

Good points, @Fernerkundung. You might be correct regarding the server load. It seems to me, though, that they run the query again for each of the 100-entry subqueries and don't do any caching, so I would assume that limiting the amount of subqueries would be beneficial for the server.

You make a good point that we already have a reasonable and effective limit in place by restricting the query to just the last 24 hours by default. I agree that this is quite effective limit by itself and I no longer think that adding hard limit to the response size would be a good idea. It would be a potential source of confusion and issues for our users without much benefit.

Regarding adding an option to also return the number of matched products, I see its main benefit in being able to easily get the available number of products for some query without needlessly stressing the server. The situation I have in mind here is something like "get the total number of products that cover country X".
Do you see this too much of an edge case to be included in the API?

You are right, there is another problem:

In order to reduce the costs of download from AWS buckets some metadata files listed [here](https://forum.sentinel-hub.com/t/changes-of-the-access-rights-to-l1c-bucket-at-aws-public-datasets-requester-pays/172) are still available from http access. However the url address of these files has recently changed to [roda.sentinel-hub.com/sentinel-s2-l1c/](https://roda.sentinel-hub.com/sentinel-s2-l1c/). The package is still trying to access these data from old address and therefore gets `Access Denied`.

A quick fix that you can do at the moment is to change the address in config file by setting
```bash
sentinelhub.config --aws_base_url https://roda.sentinel-hub.com/sentinel-s2-l1c/
```
But we are going to fix this in the new version.
